senses.definition: The act of giving up a religious faith or political belief that you once followed.
examples: The leader’s sudden apostatism shocked his followers, who had trusted his strong religious convictions.
Some writers warn that comfort and success can slowly lead to apostatism.

senses.definition: To completely give up your religion, principles, or political group.
examples: Under pressure from the government, some members chose to apostatize and deny their faith.
He refused to apostatize from his long‑held beliefs, even when it cost him his job.

senses.definition: Gave up a religion, principles, or political group that you previously followed.
examples: Several members apostatized during the conflict and joined the rival movement.
She later regretted having apostatized from the community that raised her.

senses.definition: Gives up a religion, principles, or political group that he, she, or they previously followed.
examples: When a leader apostatizes, many ordinary believers feel confused and betrayed.
If someone apostatizes from deeply held values, the change is rarely sudden.

senses.definition: Giving up a religion, principles, or political group that you previously followed.
examples: Apostatizing under fear of punishment has been a moral problem in many periods of history.
He worried that by changing his views he might seem to be apostatizing from his earlier promises.

senses.definition: An abnormal flow or leaking of a body fluid, especially bleeding such as a nosebleed.
examples: The doctor described the repeated nosebleeds as a case of nasal apostaxis.
In some plants, apostaxis appears as a sticky substance oozing from the bark.

senses.definition: To form an abscess or swollen, infected area in the body (now rare or obsolete).
examples: The wound began to apostem and caused the patient increasing pain.
If the tissue apostems, further surgical treatment may be needed.

senses.definition: To form an abscess or to become a swollen, infected area filled with pus (obsolete medical use).
examples: Without proper care, the injury could apostemate and lead to serious infection.
Doctors feared that the inflamed tissue would apostemate rather than heal cleanly.

senses.definition: Related to or affected by an abscess or a swollen, infected part of the body (obsolete medical use).
examples: The surgeon noted apostematic changes in the surrounding tissue.
Apostematic swelling in the limb signaled a serious underlying infection.

senses.definition: The process of forming an abscess or swollen, infected area in the body (obsolete medical term).
examples: Medieval texts describe apostemation as a common effect of untreated wounds.
Apostemation in the lung often proved fatal before modern antibiotics existed.

senses.definition: Having or showing abscesses; affected by swollen, infected areas (obsolete medical use).
examples: The autopsy revealed apostematous organs throughout the body.
Apostematous lesions on the skin indicated a severe systemic disease.

senses.definition: An abscess; a swollen area in the body filled with pus (obsolete medical term).
examples: The surgeon drained the aposteme to relieve the patient’s pain.
Without treatment, the small boil developed into a large aposteme.

senses.definition: Based on experience or observation, not only on logical reasoning; a variant spelling of a posteriori.
examples: The theory could be confirmed aposteriori through careful experiments.
He argued aposteriori, drawing his conclusions from real‑world cases.

senses.definition: A rare condition in which a male is born without a foreskin (technical medical term).
examples: Aposthia is sometimes mistaken for the result of circumcision because the foreskin is naturally absent.
Cases of aposthia are rarely discussed outside specialist medical literature.

senses.definition: An abscess or swollen, infected area in the body; also, by extension, a source of moral or social corruption (archaic).
examples: The physician lanced the painful aposthume on the patient’s leg.
A satirist once called the corrupt court an aposthume on the body of the nation.

senses.definition: A short note written in the margin of a document or manuscript (archaic).
examples: The scholar added an apostil in the margin to clarify the old law.
Medieval manuscripts are often full of apostils from later readers.

senses.definition: An official certificate attached to a document to show that its signature or seal is genuine, used especially for international legal documents.
examples: You may need an apostille for your birth certificate before you can use it abroad.
The lawyer explained how to obtain an apostille from the foreign ministry.

senses.definition: A note written in the margin of a document or letter (archaic).
examples: In older legal records, the clerk sometimes added an apostille to explain the decision.
The judge’s apostille in the margin criticized the earlier ruling.

senses.definition: Official certificates attached to documents to show that their signatures or seals are genuine, especially for use between different countries.
examples: The firm specializes in obtaining apostilles for corporate documents.
Several apostilles were attached to the international adoption papers.

senses.definition: Short notes written in the margins of documents or manuscripts (archaic).
examples: The treaty’s margins were crowded with later apostils and corrections.
Scholars study the apostils to understand how the text was received over time.

senses.definition: One of the original twelve followers of Jesus Christ, chosen to spread his teaching.
examples: Peter is often remembered as the leading apostle in the early church.
The painting shows each apostle gathered around Jesus at the table.

senses.definition: Someone who strongly supports a new idea, cause, or system and works hard to spread it.
examples: She became an apostle of environmental protection in her community.
He is known as an apostle of free trade in modern economics.

senses.definition: The position, office, or state of being an apostle.
examples: His sense of apostlehood included a strong duty to suffer for his message.
Writers sometimes discuss Paul’s apostlehood and how it differed from that of the Twelve.

senses.definition: The original followers of Jesus Christ who were sent out to preach his message; by extension, strong supporters of a cause.
examples: The apostles played a central part in spreading Christianity across the Roman world.
In modern times, the apostles of human rights speak out in many countries.

senses.definition: The role or work of an apostle, especially the mission of spreading a religious message.
examples: Paul defended his apostleship as a call directly from Christ.
Her quiet apostleship in the community touched many lives.

senses.definition: Roles or offices of apostles, or different forms of apostolic mission.
examples: The book compares the various apostleships described in the New Testament.
Some theologians distinguish between early and later apostleships in church history.

senses.definition: A rare or obsolete spelling variant of apostille meaning a marginal note or, in modern use, a legal authentication.
examples: The old contract included an apostoile clarifying the parties’ duties.
In some records, the word apostoile appears where later texts use apostille.

senses.definition: The work, mission, or office of an apostle, especially organized work to spread a religious faith.
examples: The missionary group focused on an apostolate of education and health care.
Lay people now play an active part in the church’s apostolate.

senses.definition: Different areas of organized religious work or mission, often carried out by church groups.
examples: Their community runs several apostolates, including schools and hospitals.
New apostolates were created to respond to the needs of migrants.

senses.definition: A rare or old‑fashioned term for a female apostle.
examples: Some legends refer to Mary Magdalene as an apostoless to the early church.
The poet pictured an unnamed apostoless preaching in the marketplace.

senses.definition: A surname or given name derived from the word apostle; also used for places or institutions named after apostles.
examples: The painting was signed by G. Apostoli, a little‑known artist of the period.
They visited San Apostoli, a church dedicated to the holy apostles.

senses.definition: Relating to an apostle or to the work and mission of an apostle (rare or archaic).
examples: The preacher’s apostolian zeal impressed even his critics.
An apostolian spirit of service guided the group’s activities among the poor.

senses.definition: Relating to the apostles, their time, their teaching, or their work.
examples: The creed has its roots in the apostolic preaching of the early church.
They tried to live in a way that reflected apostolic simplicity and generosity.

senses.definition: Relating to the pope, who is seen as the chief successor of the apostles in some churches.
examples: The letter came from the Apostolic See in Rome.
She works in a department of the apostolic administration of the church.

senses.definition: Relating to the apostles or their teaching; another form of apostolic, now less common.
examples: The writer praised the church’s return to an apostolical pattern of life.
Old confessions of faith often claim an apostolical origin.

senses.definition: In a way that follows the example, style, or mission of the apostles.
examples: She tried to live apostolically, traveling light and serving the poor.
The community seeks to act apostolically in all its projects.

senses.definition: The quality of being like an apostle in spirit, teaching, or mission.
examples: They admired the apostolicalness of his simple lifestyle and bold preaching.
Some reformers argued that true apostolicalness required poverty and service.

senses.definition: Members of certain historical religious groups or movements that claimed to follow an apostolic way of life (rare, historical).
examples: The medieval apostolici preached poverty and simplicity as essential virtues.
Church councils sometimes condemned the apostolici as heretical.

senses.definition: Belief in or emphasis on following the example and teaching of the apostles; also, systems that claim direct continuity with them.
examples: The movement’s strong apostolicism shaped its worship and leadership.
Some churches stress apostolicism through an unbroken chain of ordination.

senses.definition: Different forms or expressions of belief that stress following the apostles’ example or maintaining continuity with them.
examples: The author compares the various apostolicisms found in modern Christian groups.
These apostolicisms differ in how they understand authority and tradition.

senses.definition: Different ways or degrees in which churches or movements are considered to be apostolic in origin, teaching, or structure.
examples: The council discussed the apostolicities of various local churches.
Scholars debate the historical apostolicities claimed by newer denominations.

senses.definition: The quality of being connected with the apostles, especially through faithful teaching or an unbroken line of leaders.
examples: Many churches claim apostolicity as one mark of their true identity.
The creed describes the church as having unity, holiness, catholicity, and apostolicity.

senses.definition: To carry out work like that of an apostle, especially preaching or spreading a religious message (chiefly British, rare).
examples: He left his job to apostolise among the rural poor.
The group sought to apostolise new regions with little Christian presence.

senses.definition: Carried out work like that of an apostle, especially preaching or spreading a religious message (chiefly British, rare).
examples: For many years she apostolised in remote mountain villages.
The region had been apostolised by earlier missionaries.

senses.definition: Carries out work like that of an apostle, especially preaching or spreading a religious message (chiefly British, rare).
examples: He apostolises tirelessly in inner‑city neighborhoods.
The community apostolises through service and simple living.

senses.definition: Doing work like that of an apostle, especially preaching or spreading a religious message (chiefly British, rare).
examples: They spent years apostolising among the island communities.
Apostolising in hostile environments required courage and patience.

senses.definition: To act like an apostle, especially by preaching or spreading a religious message.
examples: He felt called to apostolize in the big cities of the region.
The group aimed to apostolize through both words and works of charity.

senses.definition: Acted like an apostle, especially by preaching or spreading a religious message.
examples: She apostolized among factory workers for more than a decade.
Earlier generations had already apostolized much of the region.

senses.definition: Acts like an apostle, especially by preaching or spreading a religious message.
examples: He apostolizes wherever he goes, speaking to anyone who will listen.
The small community apostolizes through example rather than argument.

senses.definition: Acting like an apostle, especially by preaching or spreading a religious message.
examples: They spent their youth apostolizing in small rural parishes.
Apostolizing among hostile groups demanded patience and respect.

senses.definition: A transliteration of the Greek word for apostle, used especially in religious or academic writing.
examples: The term apostolos in the New Testament can mean both messenger and commissioned leader.
Scholars debate how broadly apostolos is used in different books of the Bible.

senses.definition: Relating to or marked by an apostrophe, the punctuation mark (rare, technical).
examples: The editor corrected several apostrophal errors in the manuscript.
Apostrophal forms like it’s and who’s often cause confusion for learners.

senses.definition: The use or addition of an apostrophe in writing (rare, technical).
examples: Incorrect apostrophation can make even a good essay look careless.
The style guide gives clear rules for apostrophation in plurals and possessives.
